items.q: Which mood follows 'je souhaite que' (I wish that)?
items.why: Students use indicative after expressions of wish. SOUHAITER QUE, VOULOIR QUE, AIMER QUE all trigger subjunctive when there's a different subject. Note: espérer que typically takes indicative - a common irregularity.

items.q: How do you say 'I am using my phone right now' (emphasising the action is in progress)?
items.correct: Je suis en train d'utiliser mon portable.
items.why: Students translate English '-ing' as a participle. French has no progressive tense - the present 'j'utilise' covers both 'I use' and 'I am using'. For emphasis on the ongoing action, use 'être en train de + infinitive'.

items.q: What is the 'vous' form of 'être' in the present tense?
items.why: Students apply -ez ending from regular verbs. Être is irregular: je suis, tu es, il est, nous sommes, vous ÊTES, ils sont. Memorisation is essential - être appears constantly.

items.q: In what year was L'Etranger published?
items.why: It's 1942. “1938” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What does Meursault cite as cause for the murder?
items.why: It's The sun. “Anger” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Camus win the Nobel Prize?
items.why: Exact answer: 1957. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
